clean_text: Alhamdulillah. Continuing in the final and closing verse of surah al chapter number 73 verse number 20. This is a longer verse. It's the longest verse in the chapter. And at first glance, this verse seemingly doesn't match up with the rhythm of all of the previous verses. Meaning there's a big distinction and a very deep difference between and a very fundamental difference and distinction between the Meccan chapters and the Medini chapters. Chapters that were revealed prior to Hijra are referred to as Mecci. chapters that were revealed after the event of Hijra are referred to as Medi. Those two eras because Mecc Mecc and Medanin, as we've mentioned before, this is not a reference to a place. It's a reference to a time, an era. So, we have the the Mechi epic or era and then we have the Medani epic or era. And these two eras have very distinct verses, very distinct subject matters, very distinct very distinct context to one another. One, we have the prophet sallall.aii alaihi wasallam and his companions living as a minority amongst a majority of aggressive non-Muslims and combative non-Muslims. Then we have in Medina, they're still somewhat a minority though they were the growing major their majority was growing very fast and very quickly. But now they are no longer just a subject. They're no longer subjects to another king or another rule. Now they are at the helm of beginning their own state. And so these two eras have very differentloop. They have very different with respect to the Quran. You can see a very distinct difference in even the length of the verses, the subject matter of the verses, even the language and diction of the verses are very distinct and different. For this reason, the scholars may differ somewhat on which verses are Medani and which verses are mechi. We have a general rule, general principle and that is that when a chapter is revealed in Mecca, it starts being revealed in Mecca is that all of the chapter is Mecci. And if a chapter is revealed in Medina, all of the chapter is going to be Medi. But that is not an absolute rule. There are some chapters that are predominantly mechi but have Medani elements or verses and vice versa. There are chapters which are predominantly Medani but have what? Mechan verses. Understanding this is very important and the scholars have taken great pains to ensure documenting and scouring every corner of the books of history and the books of hadith in search of identifying exactly which chapters and which verses were revealed in which era. Why is that so important? Why is it so important that we identify what was revealed in Mecca versus what was revealed in Medina? Who wants to take a go at that? Why is that so detrimental? Or why is that so important and so of such importance that it warrants such precision from the scholarship of Islam >> good? information. >> So it's it gives it context, right? And what is the most important determining factor with respect to the meanings of words? Context. Correct? I mean, think about it. Context is more important than the actual words that are even said. Context can be more important than the actual words that are said. Let me give you an example. Let's say somebody let's say you were to ask me about a a person or you know let's say a particular thing you're to ask me about this what do you think about this do you this or you don't it and I say I love it that I love this compare Compare that to I love it. Right. Oh, I love that. You notice the difference in tone? You notice the different body language? That's context. One is taken to mean, yes, he loves that thing. The other is taken to mean exactly the opposite of what the words themselves say. It's what we call sarcasm. Correct? Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says he's saying this to the people of the hellfire as they're getting molten hellfire poured on their heads. It will be said to them, "Taste this. Indeed, you are the honorable and you are the noble. How is it? Look how the meanings are clashing here. We've got hellfire poured on his head, which is the lowest of humiliation. And then it said to him, you are the noble. You are the, you know, the things that used to be chanted by their minions to them in the dunya. This is what we call in Arabic, which is sarcasm, right? It's sarcasm. That word that that statement on its own, we wouldn't understand that if it weren't for the context. When we have a verse that is revealed in the context of Mecca, radically different connotations, radically different meanings are derived from the words of that verse as opposed to if they were revealed in Medina. Mecca and Medani is one of the most important sciences of the Quran. So much so that we don't even have that just with the Quran but we also have that with the Sunnah. But with the Sunnah it is less difficult because most of the time the prophet sallallah.aii wasallam is saying something to a person whether it's the narrator of the hadith himself that provides context or an event that is happening. So the context is usually self-evident, but in the Quran, we're not sure. We have to be given that context. And so identifying what is Mecc and what is Medani is the difference between is the it can potentially hold the same difference that our example in our analogy gave of use of sarcasm. For instance, right? If I were to say for you, if I were to say to you, , I really admire this person, and then I were to say, oh, I really admire this guy. Right? That just those ways that I'm saying it, the in intonation, the the inflection in my voice changes the entire meaning, even though the words themselves are exactly the same. Correct? That is why mechi and medin is so critical. Now in identifying mechi and medi the scholars have rules of thumb that they use. So they say okay if we were to gather all of the verses that are mechi and all of the verses that are medin that we are positive this was revealed in Mecca and this is revealed in Medina. and we were to compare those two. So for instance, we are positive it was revealed in Mecca. We are positive it was revealed as a Medani chapter. It was revealed in Mecca but during the Medani era. If we were to compare these two chapters, we would see very distinct differences. So the scholars did what? They took all the chapters that they know are Mecci for sure. There's no difference of opinion. And they took all the chapters that are Medani and are agreed upon amongst the scholarship of Islam as being Medani. And then they compared those two and came up with a couple of key differences that are found to distinguish between the Meccin and Medini. Then there's another group that is different upon. Some say it's Meccin, some say it's Medini. And so they try to apply those rules to this other group of chapters or verses that are Mech. And one of those is the length of the verses. Well, just like just like the general obligations of Islam were mentioned in Mecca and then filled out later in Medina. So for instance, things that are permissible to eat and impermissible to eat. In Mecca, Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala mentions three main things. these three, right? And then the rest is all anything that is filthy, anything that is bad for your body or anything that is right. Then when we get to Medina, then we start having the rulings of how slaughtering is to be done. And we have the rulings of the the the food of a the the slaughtered meat of a Jew or a Christian. summary: This lecture discusses Surah Al-Muzzammil Chapter 73, Verse 20, focusing on the distinction between Meccan and Medinan revelations. The speaker explains that Meccan chapters were revealed before the Hijra when Muslims were a persecuted minority, while Medinan chapters came after when Muslims established their own state. The speaker elaborates on how understanding this context is crucial for interpreting Quranic verses, as the same words can have drastically different meanings depending on their revelation context. The lecture examines why Verse 20 of Surah Al-Muzzammil is debated among scholars—some argue it's Medinan due to its length and mention of jihad and Zakat, while others contend it's Meccan with forward-looking references to obligations that would be detailed later in Medina.
suggested_title: Meccan vs Medinan Revelations: Understanding Surah Al-Muzzammil 73:20 and Quranic Context
catchy_title: Why Does This Verse Break the Pattern?
